name: Sorting
coreIntuition: Sorting isn't just about 'putting things in order'. It's about **creating structure that makes other operations efficient**. A sorted array enables binary search (O(log n) lookup), makes duplicates adjacent (easy dedup), enables two-pointer techniques, and turns many O(n²) problems into O(n log n). The real skill: recognizing when sorting the input is the first step toward a solution.
mentalModel: Sorting is a pre-processing step that transforms chaos into order. Once sorted, the array has a property: arr[i] <= arr[i+1]. This simple property unlocks: binary search, two pointers, greedy scanning, merge operations, and range queries. When facing a hard problem, ask: 'Would this be easy if the input were sorted? Can I afford to sort it?'
whenToUse: You need to find pairs/groups with specific sum or difference properties
The problem asks about median, percentiles, or relative ordering
Sorting is a preprocessing step for two pointers or binary search
You need to process items by priority (earliest deadline, highest value)
The problem involves intervals (sort by start/end time)

thinkingFramework.step: 1. Can you sort without losing information?
thinkingFramework.detail: If the answer depends on original positions, sorting destroys that information unless you sort (value, index) pairs. If original order doesn't matter, sort freely.
Sort by the attribute that reveals structure. For intervals: sort by start time (for merging) or end time (for scheduling). For events: sort by time. The choice of sort key is often the insight.

In interviews, use the language's built-in sort (O(n log n)). Know merge sort (stable, good for linked lists, enables inversion counting) and quicksort (in-place, average O(n log n)). For special cases: counting sort (O(n+k) for small value ranges).
Many problems need custom sort orders. 'Largest number from array' → compare by string concatenation order. 'Sort by frequency' → sort by count, then by value. Master custom comparators.

commonTraps.trap: Forgetting that sorting is O(n log n) — it may dominate the total complexity
commonTraps.howToAvoid: If the rest of your algorithm is O(n), sorting makes the whole thing O(n log n). That's usually fine, but verify it meets the problem's constraints.
If equal elements must retain their original relative order, use a stable sort (merge sort). Most language built-in sorts are stable (Python, Java) but C++ std::sort is not.

commonTraps.trap: Sorting when a simpler structure suffices
commonTraps.howToAvoid: For 'top K elements', you don't need to sort the whole array — use a heap (O(n log k)) or quickselect (O(n) average). Don't sort when you only need partial order.

interviewVariantStrategy: Sorting problems in interviews: (1) **Custom comparators**: 'arrange numbers to form the largest number' — compare by concatenation. (2) **Sorting as preprocessing**: sort then apply two pointers, greedy, or binary search. (3) **Non-comparison sorts**: counting sort for small ranges, radix sort for fixed-width integers. (4) **Sorting-related algorithms**: merge sort for inversion counting, quickselect for kth element. The trick: many problems that don't mention sorting become trivial after sorting. Always ask: 'What would this look like if the input were sorted?'

resources.title: VisuAlgo — Sorting Algorithms
resources.why: Watch every sorting algorithm animate step-by-step — the best way to build intuition for how each one works

resources.title: Abdul Bari — Sorting Algorithms Comparison
resources.why: Clear comparison of all major sorting algorithms with complexity analysis and when to use each

resources.title: CP-Algorithms — Sorting and Related
resources.why: Reference implementations with analysis for merge sort inversions, radix sort, and custom comparators
